examples.title: GraphPlan Algorithm
examples.description: Complete GraphPlan algorithm with expansion and solution extraction phases
examples.code:
FUNCTION GraphPlan(problem):
 // Initialize planning graph
 graph = InitialPlanningGraph(problem.initial)
 
 WHILE True:
 // Check if goals are achievable
 IF GoalsInLevel(graph, problem.goals) THEN
 // Extract solution through backward search
 solution = ExtractSolution(graph, problem.goals)
 IF solution ≠ NULL THEN
 RETURN solution
 
 // Check if graph has leveled off
 IF LeveledOff(graph) THEN
 RETURN NULL // No solution exists
 
 // Expand graph by one level
 graph = ExpandGraph(graph, problem.actions)

FUNCTION ExpandGraph(graph, actions):
 // Add action level
 prevProps = graph.lastPropositionalLevel
 newActions = []
 FOR each action IN actions:
 IF PreconditionsSatisfied(action, prevProps) THEN
 newActions.append(action)
 
 // Compute mutex relations for actions
 actionMutex = ComputeMutex(newActions)
 
 // Add propositional level with effects
 newProps = ComputeEffects(newActions, prevProps)
 
 // Compute mutex for propositions
 propMutex = ComputePropMutex(newProps)
 
 RETURN graph.addLevels(newActions, newProps, actionMutex, propMutex)

FUNCTION ExtractSolution(graph, goals):
 level = graph.lastLevel
 goalSet = goals
 
 WHILE level > 0:
 // Select actions to achieve goals (no mutex)
 actions = SelectActions(goalSet, level, graph)
 IF actions = NULL THEN
 RETURN NULL // Backtrack
 
 // Collect preconditions for next iteration
 preconditions = []
 FOR each action IN actions:
 preconditions.add(action.preconditions)
 
 level = level - 1
 goalSet = preconditions
 
 RETURN reverse(actions)
examples.explanation: GraphPlan works in two phases: (1) Expansion builds the planning graph level by level, checking mutex relations at each step; (2) Solution extraction performs backward search from goals to find a consistent plan. The algorithm terminates when goals are achievable without mutex conflicts or when the graph levels off.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × k) polynomial in graph size

examples.title: Mutex Computation
examples.code:
FUNCTION ComputeMutex(actions):
 mutexPairs = []
 
 FOR each pair (a, b) IN actions WHERE a ≠ b:
 // Check Inconsistent Effects
 IF InconsistentEffects(a, b) THEN
 mutexPairs.add((a, b))
 
 // Check Interference
 ELSE IF Interference(a, b) OR Interference(b, a) THEN
 mutexPairs.add((a, b))
 
 // Check Competing Needs
 ELSE IF CompetingNeeds(a, b) THEN
 mutexPairs.add((a, b))
 
 // Check Inverse
 ELSE IF Inverse(a, b) THEN
 mutexPairs.add((a, b))
 
 RETURN mutexPairs

FUNCTION InconsistentEffects(a, b):
 FOR each literal p IN a.addEffects:
 IF b.addEffects contains NOT(p) THEN
 RETURN True
 RETURN False

FUNCTION Interference(a, b):
 // a interferes with b if a deletes a precondition of b
 FOR each literal p IN b.preconditions:
 IF p IN a.delEffects THEN
 RETURN True
 RETURN False

FUNCTION CompetingNeeds(a, b):
 // Actions have mutually exclusive preconditions
 FOR each pair (p, q) IN a.preconditions × b.preconditions:
 IF p = NOT(q) THEN
 RETURN True
 RETURN False

FUNCTION Inverse(a, b):
 // a is inverse of b
 RETURN (a.addEffects = b.delEffects) AND (a.delEffects = b.addEffects)
examples.explanation: Four types of mutex relations are computed: (1) Inconsistent Effects when actions add contradictory literals; (2) Interference when one action deletes another's precondition; (3) Competing Needs when actions require mutually exclusive preconditions; (4) Inverse when actions are exact opposites.
